sentences.text: That's OK.
It's January 15, 1926. in Berlin, Germany, B-E-R-L-I-N.
[SIGHS] That's a painful thing to say.
My childhood was a beautiful one.
I was an only granddaughter to a large family.
Most of them lived in Poland, except for three uncles that lived in Germany, including my parents.
Unfortunately, everything was very short-lived.
Because when Hitler came to power, I was so excited about it that I came home, as a child of six, and I said, Heil Hitler.
And my poor mother didn't know how to explain that to me that this was not meant for me.
Shortly thereafter, a year later, in 1934, we were told being that my parents were of Polish origin, that we have to leave Germany.
Otherwise, we will be shipped out.
I have-- had an older brother, eight years older than myself.
And being that none in the family spoke Polish, we moved to Poland, [BACKROUND NOISE] but to a city that spoke only German.
The city was called Bielsko, Bielitz.
My parents were Orthodox Jews that lived a very comfortable life.
We had a beautiful apartment.
My father was in a company in, in Germany that was dealing with the-- he was partner there-- with linen and tablecloth.
And the life was a very comfortable and beautiful one.
It was a big Jewish community.
We lived not too far from the Jewish center, where my father belonged to an Orthodox shul.
My brother started school there.
He went to the [GERMAN]-- no, on to the-- I started at school on-- in Auguststrasse, which was also a very short time because we had to leave.
And it was very unfortunate because we lived a nice, quiet, comfortable life.
And we had relatives there.
We celebrated all the holidays together, which was very nice.
My aunt and uncle were [?
Brauner ?], [NON-ENGLISH] and [?
Hannah ?] [?
Brauner ?] and their three sons.
They survived the war.
Then I had an uncle living in Hanover.
His name was David Gross.
And an uncle in Berlin by the name of [?
Jakob ?] Gross-- he did not survive either.
My father's name was Leo Gross.
And my mother's name was Julia Gross.
My brother's name was [NON-ENGLISH].
Actually, that was his nickname, because name was David Gross.
And everybody called him [?
Arbid ?].
He was known all over by his friends by the name of [?
Arbid ?].
It was there.
But the Jews had a very nice life.
And I don't think they have accepted that.
Because until Hitler came to power, they had other problems, which they were, I suppose, mostly interested in-- that the Communist Party was very strong.
So they didn't realize that Hitler was the worst of the two evils.
And that's how the story went.
Yes.
We went-- yes.
We moved to Bielitz, which was a German-speaking city, which was a beautiful, very sports-oriented city.
And the Jews lived very, very lovely.
That was an elegant city.
There, there was-- the Hakoah was very active, Maccabi.
There was two sports clubs.
I belonged to both of them.
I had the first prize in swimming in the Hakoah for 100 meters.
I was a gymnast.
But in 1939, when the war broke out, even that has ended.
And when we came to Bielitz, it was very, very difficult for me because I didn't speak the language.
And I started going to school.
I did not understand anything.
I used to come home with high temperature because my hearing did not get used to the language.
It takes time to accept a foreign language until you, you feel comfortable with it to hear it.
Polish.
Polish-- very difficult.
Because it is a Slavic language, which is not related to anything that I knew.
Yes.
I always wanted to be a doctor.
But, unfortunately, Hitler had different plans for me.
So that had to be given up.
Because I-- first of all, I wouldn't have been able to afford it.
And I have lost the nicest part of my life, which was-- as a teenager, I was-- I have never been called on the telephone for a date.
I have never gone out on a date.
Because the childhood I have spent, instead of a healthy camp, I was in a concentration camp.
Yes, definitely so.
It was very strong Nazi stronghold.
The building that we lived in, they had all the flags, the swastika flags, up in the attic.
And the day the war broke out, on September 1, 1939, when the bombs were flying, the landlady told my mother, Mrs.
Gross, don't worry, those are ours. 'Ours,' she meant the German Luftwaffe, which certainly was not a consolation to my mother.
And on the second day of the war, which was September 2, my brother had a broken leg, and my parents couldn't move.
They gave me my bicycle.
And they told me, Gerda, get away from here.
Go as far as you can.
And I left.
And that was the end of ever having a home.
I haven't slept for six years in a bed.
I didn't even know what it means to have a home at that time.
I started to go to towards Cracow.
Cracow wouldn't let us in.
So the closest to Cracow was a city called Wieliczka, where my father's sister lived, by the name of [NON-ENGLISH].
[SIGHS] And when I arrived there-- she had also a daughter that was a little bit older than I am.
But she was very much afraid that the Germans will come in and they will rape everybody.
So she told us to leave.
Another cousin of mine that came from Cracow with a baby of four weeks-- and we ran away.
And we went as far as we could, possibly to reach the Russian border.
But the Germans caught up with us.
And that was-- we had to return.
And I returned.
[CRYING] We had to return.
By that time, my parents have arrived already from Bielitz.
And we were reunited.
Also not for a very long time because then my parents moved to Cracow.
And in Cracow, shortly thereafter, they started to put up the ghetto.
[SIGHS] Being that we didn't want to stay in the ghetto, and we were not, so to say, [NON-ENGLISH] to Cracow, we had to move back to Wieliczka.
Because they would not permit, unless you were a resident of the city, to stay in the city.
You had to move out 30 kilometers out of any big city.
So we went back to Wieliczka.
And we stayed with my aunt and uncle and her children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In [SIGHS] 1940-- this all was a short time, but it was a very cruel time and a very disoriented time for us.
Yeah, one suitcase.
How much can you get from home?
We didn't have as-- what?
My uncle came to pick my parents up with the car.
And whatever they could put on it, that's from what we lived.
And being in Wieliczka, we sold the sheet.
We traded it for milk, for bread, for butter.
And that's how we stayed there, definitely not in luxury and definitely not the way I was used to.
[BACKROUND NOISE] I slept on the floor.
My brother slept in the attic.
Because they were-- they only had one small room with two beds where my parents slept.
And I slept on the floor.
And we started to work in the [GERMAN] in Wieliczka till 1942.
Sewing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [BACKROUND NOISE] In 1942, there was the liquidation of Wieliczka.
And we were in hiding.
We were hidden in the stables of my uncle.
They have made a bunker.
But, unfortunately, there were no facilities and no water.
And after a few days, it was unbearable.
And we came out.
And one of my cousins came back from camp to tell us that the city is judenrein-- that means Jew-free-- and that we better get out and, and get someplace.
So during the night, we walked towards Biezanow, which was the closest camp that we reached.
We spoke German.
So we could communicate with the Nazis.
And they permitted us to stay there.
Yeah.
Yes.
Yes, definitely so.
My father was a big Zionist.
But the two of us, my brother and myself, we were too young.
And my parents, and-- disgusted with their relatives, who were all very comfortable.
And they said, where will you go with two children, little ones, into the world?
Move back here.
And you will start life all over again.
The world doesn't end there.
It was a very big mistake because we could have spared ourselves, the trouble, and our lives, and could have spent the years either in Palestine, at the time, or in America.
We had the money to get a certificate to move out.
But I was too young to understand.
So that was it.
You mean, in, in Poland?
Yeah.
[SIGHS] Shortly before we were going into hiding, my parents gave some of their clothes to a Polish family, and said, if we come back, you give it back to us.
And all the silver and jewelry my father buried into the ground.
And he called me one day.
And he said, come here, Gerda.
I don't know who's going to survive.
You are still a little girl.
But I want you to know, in case you come back, that you should know where to go for it.
When I came back after the war, I went there.
I tried to dig it out.
But to my biggest surprise, the only thing I found was one cover from a candelabra because everything else was dug out and stolen.
They knew that there was a-- a rich Jew was living there, my uncle.
And they expected to find, which they did.
To the camp--
--yes.
So we started  to work there.
And I worked for-- at one time, in the kitchen and then in the-- washing laundry, sewing, whatever they had to do, that we worked.
And that was also not for a very long time.
Because they moved us then to another camp, which was Prokocim.
Yes?
They're fair.
You worked hard, and you had no food.
And you slept on-- in, in barracks, and you had double-deckers, no, no mattress, no blankets.
You covered with one hand, and you-- your pillow was the other hand.
And that's how-- but as long as you could think straight-- that was the beginning yet-- you still had hope.
And you still were not destroyed mentally, even physically, at that point.
Because hunger and, and-- didn't do it to the same extent as when they started to destroy you mentally.
When they moved the, the work from place to place, and you know it had no meaning whatsoever.
And they kept saying to us, we will torture you so long until the poor ones will eat up the rich ones.
They didn't mean it money-wise.
But they wanted to destroy us mentally.
So they used us to work and to-- we had one soup a day and one slice of bread for 24 hours.
It was cold.
And they were shooting.
And they were killing.
And they were torturing.
But all that, at that point yet, you could cope with.
As you went on and months turned into years, and every year-- it lost completely meaning.
You didn't know what month it is.
You didn't know what day it is.
You-- the only thing that you wanted is either to survive or to be killed not by a German gun.
My biggest dream was that I should be killed by an American bomb, that I shouldn't give them the satisfaction that they have killed me.
And I was still a little girl.
[BACKROUND NOISE] I had two long braids, a slim child.
But I was tall, and I spoke German.
And that helped me.
In Biezanow.
And then we went to Prokocim, which also was for a very short time because they switched around.
They did not want you to be too comfortable in any place.
Because then you get too familiar with your surroundings, that you could make arrangements to escape, which happened to a lot of people that tried to escape.
Some were.
Some were not because then they put in barbed wire around the camp.
So some of the people, they're hanging on the wire, electrocuted.
But we had a young doctor there, a woman doctor, and a whole staff of medical people.
She had connections, one German.
And she wanted to try and smuggle out a letter that was supposed to go to Switzerland.
And they caught that letter because she wanted to escape.
And that same day, they came.
And they took all the sick people out of the barracks into the open field.
And they shot one by one, and the doctor, and all the people that were working with her.
It was the most horrible night that you can imagine because the people were undressed naked in your presence, and one by one, were taken out.
There always was an open grave outside because they shouldn't have to be bothered.
So they just pushed you in.
Some of them, yes.
We had a lawyer there and his wife.
And their two children were hidden in a Polish family outside of the camp.
And they wanted to escape.
And they were caught.
So they were undressed in our presence, in the barracks, and taken out, and two minutes later, you know, they were shot.
But it didn't go so quick.
They tortured us for hours.
Slowly, she had to undress, and they were talking to her about her family.
And then they took her out and  shot her.
[SIGHS] The following weeks, most probably, the, the, the Polish family found out that those parents were killed.
We saw those children going around by the camp, begging for a piece of bread.
Because they threw them out.
There was a little boy and a little girl.
And I don't know what happened to them.
But their name was [?
Hoffmann. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After the doctor was killed, then all her helpers, also doctors-- I couldn't go out for-- to the lavatory, which was a outhouse, for weeks.
Because the pieces of brain and then skull was lying out on the field, which they never buried.
And the shock-- we were friends with those people.
Every inmate was your relative because you had nobody.
And you tried to support-- like a support group, you tried to talk to each other.
We will survive.
We will do.
We will-- if you lost your sense of humor, you had lost everything.
So we were entertaining each other while we were sitting on the barracks killing the lice.
We were talking to each other.
I'm inviting you for coffee.
I made good cheesecake.
It sounds ridiculous today.
But that was maybe what kept us going, that you had the will to hope that this is not the end of everything, that we will be able to continue.
Yes.
But you couldn't give up because there was nothing that you could kill yourself.
A pencil was immediate death sentence.
You used the-- in order to eat, to get that wonderful soup that you got that day, you had an old can that somebody threw out, or whatever, that you found, or a dish, or whatever.
But you had no knives, no forks, no-- you, you lived like an animal.
And the winters were very, very cold.
So if you had water in the barracks, it froze.
If somebody had to go to the bathroom at night, they wouldn't dare go out of the barracks because they would shoot you.
So they used that same can as your bathroom.
God forbid if it had a hole.
Then whoever slept on the bottom part of the double-decker, you got the shower.
It was just paradise on earth.
But you still had the will, and you still wanted to-- not to give in and to survive.
And even with your wealthy upbringing--
Yes.
I was brought up with a nanny.
I never had to wash a pair of socks.
I didn't even know what it is-- means to wash.
Somehow you picked up-- the, the strength to survive is very strong.
You don't give in.
Because if you do, you're lost.
And, also, you didn't admit to yourself that the rest of the world is going to [BACKROUND NOISE] be silent and that your family is going to be killed.
You had great hopes that-- in, in society.
You couldn't believe that the people of the book like the Germans, the educated people, will be able to-- be capable of doing what they have done.
But you still had hope that the world around us will be there to answer your prayers.
I wasn't so much hoping that my parents would come and help me.
I was hoping that they will survive, that somehow-- I was praying to God that if my father has to come back a cripple, he should just come back.
And I adored my brother.
My brother was the, the most capable human being God ever created.
He gave him every talent, but he didn't give him muscle.
He had a voice that any artist in the United States can't compare with him.
He was an artist.
He painted.
As a young fellow, in Poland, Bielitz was a textile manufacturing city.
He had connections.
And he was doing designs for British companies, textile companies, and they used his designs to manufacture material.
He was very, very talented.
God knows why he did it.
Who knows?
Well, did you know what happened to him during the war?
No, I don't.
He was picked in Wieliczka yet.
I'm going back and forth-- why I'm going back to it.
In Wieliczka, there, when they made the city judenrein, they picked certain people out that they should go to another city to run the [GERMAN] in Bochnia.
And, in the meantime, as they were--
Bochnia is a city.
It's workshops.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the way to Bochnia-- there was a fire in Bochnia.
And they called it sabotage, that.
And they took all those people, the whole trainload, and shipped them.
And nobody knows what happened to them.
They were all killed.
I don't know.
To this day, I'm searching for him.
Whenever I see a group of people, whether it's in the movies, whether it's a gathering, I always look.
Maybe I will find him.
Because he still might have the same name.
But I have changed mine because I am married.
And I'm in search of him.
I think I will search to the rest of my days.
After Prokocim, we went to Plaszow.
And Plaszow was called Julag Number I.
That's called Juden Lager Number I, which was a, a subsidiary to the camp that was called also Plaszow, where Goeth was in charge.
Goeth couldn't stand it that there is another camp.
And he wanted to get rid of this-- the head of the camp by the name of Muller.
And he tried and manipulated his way to liquidate that camp and have us all brought up to the other Plaszow.
